content.full_text:
California
SUPERSEDED
Fair Political
IN PART
Practices Comrnission
b,t I- q4-o(5
August 15, 199 O
I.Iary Jo Levinger
Town Attorney
Town of Los Gatos
P. O. Box 949
Los Gatos, CA 95031
Re: Your Request for Advice
our Fil-e No. A-90-422
6ear tts. Levinger:
You have requested advice on behal,f of councilmembers Thomas
J. Ferrito, Brent N. ventura and Joanne Benjanin concerning ap-
plication of the confl ict-of- interest provisions of the Po1itical
Reform Act (the 'Act'1.r 
The following advice is based upon the
facts provided in your letter and our telephone conversation on
July 12, L99O .
OUEST TON
Is there a relationship between the Jer.rish connunity center
(irJccn) and the Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose
( rtFederationtt) such that a gift of travet from the Federation is
also considered a gift fron the JCc?
CONCLUSI ON
The Jcc and the Federation are separate and autonomous
501(c) (3) non-profit, charitable organizations. Therefore, the
Federation was the sole donor of a gift of travel to each of the
councilmenbers. Councj,hnembers Ferrito, Ventura and Benjamin may
participate in the land use permit decision, in which the JCC is
the sole apPlicant.
FACTS
The Jewish Comnunity center, which is l-ocated in Los Gatos,
is seeking apProval from the Tohln counciL of Los Gatos for a
building ina- use perrnit to expand its facilities 
by constructing a
1 Go.r".r,."nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. commission
i-gutations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations. Section
ia6oo, et seq-.- AII- references to regulations are to Titl-e 2,
pivision e of the california code of Regulations'
428J Street,suite 8OO . P.O. Box8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916)322'5660


File No. A-9O-422
Page 2
g)rmnasium and fitness center. The permits lrere denied by the
Planning Cornmission and the Jcc is now appealing to the Torrn
Council. The hearing on the appeal wil,1 be on september 4, 1990.
Councilmembers Ferrito, Ventura and Benjanin attended the
International conference of Mayors in Jerusalem, Israel as g:uests
of the Federation. Councilmembers Ferrito and Benjamin attended
the conference in ApriL, 1989; councilnember ventura attended the
conference in Apri1, 1990.
In previous advice to !tr. Papo, $re deternined that no linits
applied to the Federation's gifts of three trips to Israel, which
were each valued at $2,100. (Papo Advice Letter, No. A-89-636. )
Horrever, it was advised that each elected officeholder woul-d have
disclosure and disqual ification obligations with respect to his or
her gift.
Both the Federation and the JCC are separate 501(c) (3)
nonprofit, tax-exenpt charitable organizations hthich provide
services to both the Jewish and non-Jewish conrnunities in the
creater san Jose area. The Federati-on and the Jcc are
incorporated separately, and have separate and autonomous
executj.ve dj.rectors, staffs, and governing boards. Each organiza-
tion has independent operating policies and the funds of each
organization are maintained separately.
The Federation Board consists of 36 directors and the Jcc
Board consists of 23 directors. 
Both boards are separate and
autononous, and each board has its own slate of officers.
Mr. Hudson, the President of the JCC, has a seat on the Federation
Board, as do all the presidents of the beneficiary agencies. The
Jcc is a beneficiary of both the Federation and the United way
campaigns.
For fiscal year 1989-90, the Federation's operating budget
was $1,685,000, srith the Jcc receiving $142,000. For fiscal year
l99o-9]- , the Federation's operating budget is projected at
12,L86,354, and the Jcc has been allocated $167,000. For fiscal
year 1989-90, the JCC had an annual operating budget of ap-
proxirnately il ,292,000; the noney received fron the Federation
represented 9 percent of the JCc's operating budget.
The Federation leases of f j.ce space from the Jcc, which is the
sole owner of the property.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official 
frorn naking, participat-
i.ng in, or using his official 
position to influence a governmentaL
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable fron its effect on the public generally, on the


File No. A-90-422
Page 3
official 
or a member of his irunediate fanily or on, among other
things :
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.
(b) Any reaL property in which the public
official 
has a direct or i.ndirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1000) or more.
(c) Any source of i.ncome, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms avaitable to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty doI-
- 
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
' 
by or proroised to the public official within 12
months prior to the tine when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
officiaL is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official 
wj.thin 12 months prior to the tine when
the decision is nade.
Section 87103(a)-(e).
The three councilmenbers are public officials. 
(section
82048.) Therefore, they may not participate in a decision if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any donor of a gift of 9250 or more within 12
months prior to a decision. (Section 87103 (e).)
Both councilrnembers Ferrito and Benjanin traveled to Israel
in April, 1989. Since this gift of travel was received more than
12 months ago, Section 87103(e) is inapplicable, regardless of who
was the donor of the gift, 
and they nay participate in the deci-
sion.
source of the Gift
since the question has been raised regarding a perceived
reLationship between the Federation and the Jcc, it nust be
deternined if the gift of travet from the Federation is also
considered a gift fron the JCC.
Previous Comrnission opinions (In re Kahn (L976) 2 FPPC oPs.
151 and rn re Lunsdon (1-97 6) 2 FPPC oPs. 140) analyzed related


File No. A-9O-422
Page 4
business entities, such as corporations and subsidiaries, for
purposes of reporting contributions. 
These opinions focused on
lrhether one of the entities exercised control over the other enti-
ties in the making of campaign contributions. without the element
of control, the contributions lrere deemed to be Dade independently
and, therefore, were not aggregated.
Based upon the facts provided, and by applying the rationale
in Kahn and Lumsdon, the Jcc and the Federation are not r'relatedrr
organizations. The Jcc and the Federation are incorporated
separately; they have separate and autonomous executive directors,
governing boards, operating policies, funds and staffs. 
one
entity does not have control over the operation or decisions of
the other, nor is there shared management and control between the
two entities.
Since the Jcc and the Federation are totally separate
organizations, the Federation's gift of travel to the three
councilmembers is a gift solelv fron the Federation. Since the
Jcc is the sole applicant before the To$rn councj.t, the conflict-
of-interest provisions are not applicable to any of the
councilmembers.
I trust this answers your questions. If you have any further
questions regarding this natter, please contact ne at
(eL6) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Actinq ceneral counsel
By:
。'StecheF
1, Legal Division

cc: Uichael A. PaPo

the sole apPlicant.
